elements.sent-2411-001: Most model reduction methods are space-only in that they reduce the spatial dimension of the solution but not the temporal one.
elements.sent-2411-002: These methods integrate an encoding of the state of the nonlinear dynamical system forward in time.
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose a space-time method --- one that solves a system of algebraic equations for the encoding of the trajectory, i.e., the solution on a time interval $[0,T]$.
elements.sent-2411-004: The benefit of this approach is that with the same total number of degrees of freedom, a space-time encoding can leverage spatiotemporal correlations to represent the trajectory far more accurately than a space-only one.
elements.sent-2411-005: We use spectral proper orthogonal decomposition (SPOD) modes, a spatial basis at each temporal frequency tailored to the structures that appear at that frequency, to represent the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-006: These modes have a number of properties that make them an ideal choice for space-time model reduction.
elements.sent-2411-007: We derive an algebraic system involving the SPOD coefficients that represent the solution, as well as the initial condition and the forcing.
elements.sent-2411-008: The online phase of the method consists of solving this system for the SPOD coefficients given the initial condition and forcing.
elements.sent-2411-009: We test the model on a Ginzburg-Landau system, a $1 + 1$ dimensional nonlinear PDE.
elements.sent-2411-010: We find that the proposed method is $\sim 2$ orders of magnitude more accurate than POD-Galerkin at the same number of modes and CPU time for all of our tests.
elements.sent-2411-011: In fact, the method is substantially more accurate even than the projection of the solution onto the POD modes, which is a lower bound for the error of any space-only Petrov-Galerkin method.
elements.sent-2411-012: The great majority of model reduction methods use the following two-step approach: (i) find an efficient encoding of the state of the dynamical system, making a high dimensional system a low dimensional one, then (ii) derive a dynamical system for the low dimensional system.
elements.sent-2411-013: There are many methods for both of these steps.
elements.sent-2411-014: Proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) modes \cite{Lumley67,Sirovich87 are perhaps the most common choice for (i), but other choices include balanced truncation modes \cite{Moore81,Willcox02,Rowley05 and nonlinear encodings like those based on (nonlinear) autoencoders \cite{Choi19 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: These methods all make use of correlations between the points that make up the state.
elements.sent-2411-016: Common choices for (ii) include methods like Galerkin \cite{Aubry88,Noack03 and Petrov-Galerkin \cite{Carlberg10 projections which derive the reduced-order model (ROM) equations from the full-order model (FOM) equations, and methods like operator inference, which obtain equations for the evolution of the encoding directly from data.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The former methods are referred to as intrusive, and the latter as non-intrusive.
elements.sent-2411-018: We refer to methods that use this two-step paradigm as space-only model reduction methods --- only the spatial dimension of the system is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-019: Far less common are space-time model reduction methods \cite{Yano14,Choi19,Choi21,Choi21b,Parish21,Hoang22 , which reduce time in addition to space.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Step (i) is now to find an efficient encoding for the entire trajectory of the state over some time interval of interest.
elements.sent-2411-021: With the entire evolution of the state represented in the encoding, the degrees of freedom that represent the solution no longer evolve in time, and step (ii) becomes solving for these degrees of freedom either by solving a system of simultaneous equations or an optimization.
elements.sent-2411-022: The potential advantage of the space-time paradigm over the space-only one is that solutions to problems of interest exhibit spatiotemporal coherent structures, and these structures offer an extremely efficient means of describing the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-023: In a space-only ROM, one is nonetheless solving for an encoding of the trajectory --- the space-only encoding of the state at all time steps may be viewed as such -- but this trajectory encoding is far from optimal.
elements.sent-2411-024: Indeed, the space-only POD coefficients, for example, are highly correlated from one time step to the next, so they provide a somewhat redundant (i.e., suboptimal) encoding of the entire trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-025: Thus, with the same total degrees of freedom, the space-time framework offers the potential for substantial additional accuracy by leveraging spatiotemporal correlations.
elements.sent-2411-026: The key questions are the following.
elements.sent-2411-027: To what extent does this persist for a space-time approach? (2) Can the system of equations in the space-time ROM be solved in similar time to the time integration required in the space-only ROM? In other words, does the space-time approach take the same CPU time as the space-only approach for the same number of degrees of freedom? If the answer to both of these is `yes', then the space-time ROM will be much more accurate than a space-only ROM at the same CPU time.
elements.sent-2411-028: The spatiotemporal basis vectors used in many space-time model reduction methods are separable, i.e., they are formed by taking the Kronocker product of a spatial vector and a temporal one, and the spatial basis vectors used are often the standard POD modes \cite{Choi19,Choi21,Choi21b,Hoang22 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In most cases within these works, the time dependence for a given POD mode is tailored to that mode, which is accomplished by taking the SVD of a data matrix containing as its columns different temporal evolutions of the associated POD coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-030: In other cases, \cite{Choi19 , the same temporal basis is used for each POD mode, but in either case, the temporal basis is obtained empirically.. Refs.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Our approach is to use spectral proper orthogonal decomposition (SPOD) modes as the space-time basis.
elements.sent-2411-032: At each temporal frequency, there is a spatial basis of modes --- the SPOD modes --- that are optimal for describing the structures that appear at that temporal frequency.
elements.sent-2411-033: The modes at frequency $\omega_k$ are associated, by definition, with the time dependence $e^{i\omega_kt}$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Therefore, the SPOD modes can be used as a (separable) space-time basis by adding these modes with their associated time dependence together with constant coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-035: A number of properties of the SPOD basis make them attractive for model reduction.
elements.sent-2411-036: First, for statistically stationary systems, SPOD modes are nearly the optimal linear space-time encoding for long time intervals.
elements.sent-2411-037: More precisely, for statistically stationary systems, SPOD modes approach space-time POD modes, the optimal linear encoding of trajectories, as the time interval of the encoding goes to infinity \cite{Lumley67,Towne2018spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Second, various properties of the analytic time dependence of the modes are useful; one of particular importance is that the Fourier time dependence leads to sparser coupling via the nonlinearity than one would get with a general space-time basis.
elements.sent-2411-039: Finally, the SPOD modes are easier to obtain from data and consume less memory than other space-time modes, such as space-time POD modes.
elements.sent-2411-040: In our previous work \cite{Frame24 , we proposed a space-time ROM based on SPOD modes for linear dynamical systems, building on Refs.. \cite{Lin19,Towne21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We showed that this method, which we refer to as the linear SPOD Petrov-Galerkin (SPOD-PG) method, achieved orders-of-magnitude lower error than POD-Galerkin, balanced truncation \cite{Moore81 , and the projection of the FOM solution onto POD modes.}
elements.sent-2411-042: At a high level, the linear SPOD-PG method is based on a relation for each frequency of the Fourier transform of a trajectory of a linear system, which in turn is derived from the fundamental solution to a forced linear system.
elements.sent-2411-043: The linear SPOD-PG method obtains the SPOD coefficients by projecting this relation onto the SPOD modes, and the low-error results can be understood as the fact that there is minimal error introduced both steps of this process.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this paper, we aim to extend the SPOD-PG method to nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-045: Three new aspects must be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-046: To do this, we treat the nonlinear term as though it is an additional forcing on the system and use the fundamental solution to the linear system with this additional forcing.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is consistent with an increasingly popular perspective in the fluid dynamics community on the role of nonlinearity \cite{McKeon10,McKeon17 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Crucially, this additional forcing depends on the solution itself, and this results in a system of nonlinear equations that must be solved for the SPOD coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-049: We use two approaches, depending on the nature of the nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-050: The first is a DEIM-based hyper-reduction approximation of the nonlinearity, and the second, which is only applicable for quadratic nonlinearities, uses a subset of the triadic interactions between the SPOD modes.
elements.sent-2411-051: We use a simple fixed-point iteration and find that for most cases, this converges in $\sim 10$ iterations.
elements.sent-2411-052: For cases where this does not converge, we use a pseudotime stepping method, which is somewhat slower, but more stable.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our approach may be related to harmonic balance \cite{Hall02 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: This method derives a relation between the various frequencies of a temporally periodic solution to the Navier-Stokes equations, and solves this relation --- a system of nonlinear algebraic equations --- for these frequency components.
elements.sent-2411-055: This has been applied in turbomachinery problems \cite{Hall02,Hall13 , where the solution is assumed to be periodic with the fundamental frequency given by the blade-passing frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-056: It has also been used to study boundary layer transition \cite{rigas21 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In both, few harmonics are retained, and \cite{rigas22 has proposed closures for unretained frequencies.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Our method may be viewed as a spatially reduced harmonic balance method, however, it is more general in that the solution need not be periodic.
elements.sent-2411-059: We test the method on two Ginzburg-Landau systems, one with the standard cubic nonlinearity and the other with a Burgers'-type nonlinearity used to test the triadic-interaction handling of the nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-060: We get good results for both: at similar cost to POD-Galerkin, the SPOD-PG method is orders-of-magnitude more accurate than POD-Galerkin the projection of the solution onto the POD modes.
elements.sent-2411-061: We test the method both on statistically stationary forcings, where the SPOD modes provide the asymptotically optimal encoding, and on non-stochastic forcings, observing similar results for both.
elements.sent-2411-062: We also test the ability of the method to handle new systems by using SPOD modes from one Ginzburg-Landau system to build the ROM and then test it on a different Ginzburg-Landau system.
elements.sent-2411-063: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-064: In Section~\ref{sec:preliminaries , we describe trajectory representation, SPOD modes, and the linear SPOD-PG method.}
elements.sent-2411-065: We derive the (general) SPOD-PG method in Section~\ref{sec:method .}
elements.sent-2411-066: We report the results of the application of the method to the Ginzburg-Landau system(s) in Section~\ref{sec:results , and finally conclude the paper in Section~\ref{sec:conclusions .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Spectral POD and the linear SPOD-PG method
elements.sent-2411-067: In this section, we discuss the preliminaries that lay the foundation for the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-068: First, we discuss the task of trajectory encoding.
elements.sent-2411-069: Then, we review the relevant properties of spectral POD.
elements.sent-2411-070: The most important of these is the way SPOD modes can be used to represent a trajectory and the efficiency of this representation.
elements.sent-2411-071: Finally, we review the linear SPOD-PG method \cite{Frame24 , which is the antecedent to this work.}
elements.sent-2411-072: A common task in model reduction is formulating an accurate scheme for encoding the state of a dynamical system $\vd{q \in \mathbb{C ^{N_x}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: This entails specifying an encoder $\vd{e ^{x : \mathbb{C ^{N_x \to \mathbb{C ^r$ and a decoder $\vd{d ^x: \mathbb{C ^{r \to \mathbb{C ^{N_x}$, where, ideally, $r \ll N_x$ and $\vd{q \approx \vd{d ^x(\vd{e ^x(\vd{q ))$ for some appropriate ensemble of states $\vd{q}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The superscript indicates that the spatial dimension is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-075: It is well known that, in a certain precise sense, the first $r$ proper orthogonal decomposition modes can be used to formulate the optimal linear encoding scheme.
elements.sent-2411-076: Specifically, the encoder $\vd{e ^{x (\vd{q ) = \td{\Phi ^*\td{W \vd{q}$ and decoder $\vd{d ^{x (\vd{a ) = \td{\Phi}\vd{a}$ minimize $\mathbb{E [\| \vd{q - \vd{d ^x(\vd{e ^x(\vd{q )) \|^2]$ over all linear encoder/decoder pairs \cite{Oja82 , where $\td{\Phi \in \mathbb{C ^{N_x \times r}$ is the matrix of the first $r$ POD modes, and $\td{W}$ is a (positive definite) weight matrix.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The norm is induced by the inner product $\| \vd{q \| = \sqrt{\langle \vd{q , \vd{q \rangle}$, the inner product is defined using the weight matrix as $\langle \vd{q _1, \vd{q _2 \rangle = \vd{q _2^* \td{W \vd{q _1$, and $\mathbb{E [ \cdot ]$ is the expectation operator over the ensemble that defines the POD modes.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: A related, though less familiar, task is formulating an encoding / decoding scheme for trajectories of the state of the dynamical system on some time interval $[0,T]$.
elements.sent-2411-079: We take the trajectory on the interval to be a vector of its values at times $0, \Delta t, \dots, (N_\omega-1)\Delta t$, where $N_\omega \Delta t = T$.
elements.sent-2411-080: That is, the trajectory is a vector $\vd{q _\set{J = [\vd{q _0^T, \vd{q _1^T, \dots, \vd{q _{N_\omega - 1 ^T]^T$ where $\vd{q _j$ is the state at time $t_j = j \Delta T$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Throughout the paper, a subsript $\set{J}$ is used to indicate the set or vector that is formed by taking the subsript to be each element of the vector $\set{J = [0, 1, \dots, N_\omega - 1]^T$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: For example $t_\set{J}$ denotes the vector $[0,\Delta t, \dots, (N_\omega - 1)\Delta t]^T$.
elements.sent-2411-083: We denote the number of time steps $N_\omega$ for reasons related to the discrete Fourier transform, which will become clear later.
elements.sent-2411-084: An encoding scheme for trajectories comprises an encoder $\vd{e ^{x,t : \mathbb{C ^{N_xN_\omega \to \mathbb{C ^{rN_\omega}$ and a decoder $\vd{d ^{x,t _\set{J : \mathbb{C ^{rN_\omega \to \mathbb{C ^{N_xN_\omega}$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: The encoding scheme is effective if $\vd{q _\set{J \approx \vd{d ^{x,t _\set{J (\vd{e ^{x,t (\vd{q _\set{J ))$ for trajectories of the system.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: More precisely, the expected error averaged over the trajectory $\mathbb{E [ \| \vd{q _\set{J - \vd{d ^{x,t _\set{J (\vd{e ^{x,t (\vd{q _\set{J )) \|_{x,t ^2]$ should be small, where the space-time square norm is \begin{equation}\| \vd{q}_\set{J} \|_{x,t}^2 = \frac{1}{N_\omega} \sum_{j =0}^{N_\omega - 1} \| \vd{q}_j \|^2 \text{.}\end{equation} The analog of POD for the case of trajectories is space-time POD \cite{Lumley67,Frame23 --- it is the optimal linear encoding / decoding scheme for trajectories in the sense that it minimizes $\mathbb{E [ \| \vd{q _\set{J - \vd{d ^{x,t _\set{J (\vd{e ^{x,t (\vd{q _\set{J )) \|_{x,t ^2]$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: As discussed in the following subsection, SPOD mdoes can be used to represent trajectories, and a primary motivation for this paper is that the SPOD representation approaches the accuracy of the space-time POD representation for statistically stationary systems as $T \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Another important point of comparison is the POD representation of trajectories, wherein each time step of the trajectory is encoded using POD modes, i.e., where $\vd{d ^{x,t _j(\vd{e ^{x,t (\vd{q _\set{J )) = \td{\Phi}\td{\Phi ^*\td{W \vd{q _j$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Spectral POD can be viewed as a `POD of the frequency domain' for statistically stationary flows: for every temporal frequency, there is a basis of SPOD modes that optimally represent the spatial structures trajectories exhibited at that frequency.
elements.sent-2411-090: To make this precise, we define the trajectory at frequency $\omega_k$ using the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) as \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:DFT} \hat{\vd{q}}_k = \text{DFT}_k\left[ \vd{q}_\set{J} \right] = \sum_{j = 0}^{N_\omega - 1} \vd{q}_j e^{-i\omega_k t_j} \text{,}\end{equation} where $\omega_k = 2k\pi/T$.
elements.sent-2411-091: If the DFT is applied to a new trajectory of the same system (or a different interval of the same long trajectory), the Fourier components will likely be different --- if all $\hat{\vd{q _k$ were the same, the trajectories would be identical.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The SPOD modes optimally capture this variability just as the POD modes capture the variability of $\vd{q}$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Specifically, the SPOD modes at frequency $\omega_k$ can be defined to maximize the statistical energy captured at frequency $\omega_k$, which is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:opimization} \lambda_k(\vd{\psi}) = \frac{\mathbb{E}\big[ | \langle \vdh{q}_k, \vd{\psi} \rangle |^2 \big]}{\| \vd{\psi}\|^2 } \text{,}\end{equation} The first SPOD mode at frequency $\omega_k$ is defined to maximize \eqref{eq:SPOD:opimization , and the latter modes are defined to maximize the same function subject to orthogonality with respect to previous modes, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \vd{\psi}_{k,m} = \argmax \lambda_k(\vd{\psi}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \langle \vd{\psi}_{k,m} , \vd{\psi}_{k,n} \rangle = \delta_{mn} \quad \text{for} \quad 1 \leq m \leq n \text{,} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\vd{\psi _{k,m}$ denotes the $m$-th mode at the $k$-th frequency.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: We denote the energies of the modes as $\lambda_{k,m = \lambda_k(\vd{\psi _{k,m )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Note the similarity to (space-only) proper orthogonal decomposition: the SPOD modes at $\omega_k$ are to $\vdh{q _k$ as the POD modes are to $\vd{q}$.}
elements.sent-2411-096: With the modes defined, $\hat{\vd{q _k$ may be represented approximately by a linear combination of the first $r_k$ of them, \begin{equation}\hat{\vd{q}}_k \approx \td{\Psi}_k \vd{a}_k \text{,}\end{equation} Here, $\td{\Psi _k = [\vd{\psi _{k1 , \vd{\psi _{k2 , \dots, \vd{\psi _{kr_k ] \in \mathbb{C ^{N_x \times r_k}$ is the truncated matrix of modes at frequency $\omega_k$ and $\vd{a _k \in \mathbb{C ^{r_k}$ is the vector of expansion coefficients.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: It will become clear below why it is advantageous to retain different numbers of modes at different frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-098: The error in the representation can be expressed in terms of the truncated SPOD energies as \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:ex_err_w} \mathbb{E}[ \| \vdh{q}_k - \SPOD{k} \vd{a}_k \|^2] = \sum_{m = r_k + 1}^{N_x} \lambda_{k,m} \text{,}\end{equation} and the first $r_k$ SPOD modes minimize this quantity over all rank-$r_k$ bases.
elements.sent-2411-099: If $\hat{\vd{q _k$ is known, the SPOD coefficients may be found using \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:coefs_from_qhat} \vd{a}_k = \td{\Psi}_k^{*} \td{W} \hat{\vd{q}}_k \text{.}\end{equation} This may be written in terms of a space-time encoder $\vd{a _k = \vd{e ^{x,t _k(\vd{q _\set{J )$, where the $k$-th frequency of the encoder is given by \begin{equation}\vd{e}^{x,t}_k(\vd{q}_\set{J}) = \td{\Psi}_k^{*} \td{W} \ \text{DFT}_k[\vd{q}_\set{J}] \text{.}\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Our interest in using SPOD modes stems from the fact that they provide a very accurate representation of trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-101: The SPOD modes at each frequency form an approximate representation of the trajectory in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-102: Accordingly, they can be used to give an approximate space-time representation of the trajectory in the time domain by taking an inverse DFT, \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:SPOD_st_rep} \tilde{\vd{q}}_j= \frac{1}{N_\omega}\sum_{k = 0}^{N_\omega-1} \SPOD{k} \vd{a}_k e^{i\omega_k t_j} \text{.}\end{equation} where $\vdt{q _\set{J}$ is the approximate trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-103: It may be written in terms of a space-time decoder as $\vdt{q _\set{J = \vd{d ^{x,t _\set{J (\vd{a _\set{K )$, where the $j$-th time of the decoder is given by \begin{equation}\vd{d}^{x,t}_{j}(\vd{a}_\set{K}) = \frac{1}{N_\omega}\sum_{k = 0}^{N_\omega-1} \SPOD{k} \vd{a}_k e^{i\omega_k t_j} \text{.}\end{equation} Hereafter, we use a subscript $\set{K}$ to denote all frequencies.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: The relevant metric for accuracy is the spatial error averaged over the times in the interval, which is the square of the space-time norm of the difference.
elements.sent-2411-105: Due to Parseval's theorem, this is proportional to the error summed over frequencies, \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:sterror} \| \vdt{q}_\set{J} - \vd{q}_\set{J} \|^2_{x,t} = \frac{1}{N_\omega^2}\sum_{k = 0}^{N_\omega -1} \| \SPOD{k}\vd{a}_k - \vdh{q}_k \|^2 \text{.}\end{equation} Taking the expected value of both sides and using \eqref{eq:SPOD:ex_err_w , we have \begin{equation}\mathbb{E} \left[ \| \vdt{q}_\set{J} - \vd{q}_\set{J} \|^2_{x,t} \right] = \frac{1}{N_\omega^2} \sum_{k = 0}^{N_\omega -1} \sum_{m = r_k + 1}^{N_x} \lambda_{k,m} \text{.}\end{equation} Thus, the expected accuracy of the trajectory representation is determined by the energies of the truncated SPOD modes.}
elements.sent-2411-106: This informs the best allocation of modes across frequencies: for the best accuracy given a total number of modes, one should retain the modes with the highest energies over all frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-107: We denote the average number of modes retained by $r$, and denote the $j$-th largest SPOD energy at any frequency by $\tilde{\lambda _j$.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The number of modes retained at the $k$-th frequency $r_k$ is then the number of modes at this frequency with energy greater or equal to the $\tilde{\lambda _{rN_\omega}$, \begin{equation}r_k = |\{ m: \lambda_{k,m} \geq \tilde{\lambda}_{rN_\omega} \}| \text{.}\end{equation} The SPOD trajectory representation \eqref{eq:SPOD:SPOD_st_rep may be viewed as a rank-$rN_\omega$ space-time representation, i.e., it is a sum of spatiotemporal modes with constant coefficients of the form $\sum_{i = 1 ^{rN_\omega \vd{\xi _i a_i$ with $\vd{\xi _i \in \mathbb{C ^{N_\omega N_x}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In the SPOD representation, each spatiotemporal mode is an SPOD mode at some frequency $\omega_k$ multiplied by the associated oscillatory time dependence $e^{i\omega_k t}$.
elements.sent-2411-110: The most accurate space-time representation is space-time POD modes --- these lead to lower trajectory representation error than any other space-time basis.
elements.sent-2411-111: The fact that motivates our use of SPOD modes is that as the time interval $T \to \infty$, the SPOD modes approach space-time POD modes \cite{Lumley67,Lumley70,Frame23 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: This limit is approached fairly quickly in practice, so when $T$ is long compared to the correlation time of the system, the SPOD modes provide a representation of trajectories that is nearly optimally accurate given the total number of coefficients used (see Figure 2 in Ref. \cite{Frame24 ).}

elements.sent-2411-118: The goal of the of the paper is to develop a reduced order model for nonlinear systems that solves for the SPOD coefficients quickly and accurately, so as to recover the accuracy shown in Figure~\ref{fig:rep_err at low cost.}

elements.sent-2411-287: Throughout the remainder of this section, the (relative) errors are defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-288: The error at time $t_j$ is the square norm of the difference from the FOM solution normalized by the mean square norm of the FOM solution over time and over test trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-289: This may be written as \begin{equation}\label{results:err_t} e_j = \frac{\| \vdt{q}_j - \vd{q}_j \|^2}{\sum_{i = 1}^{N_{test}} \| \vd{q}^{i}_\set{J} \|_{x,t}^2} \text{,}\end{equation} where $\vd{q}$ is the FOM solution and $\vdt{q}$ is the ROM approximation thereof.
elements.sent-2411-290: This error averaged over time may be written \begin{equation}e = \frac{\| \vdt{q}_\set{J} - \vd{q}_\set{J} \|_{x,t}^2}{\sum_{i = 1}^{N_{test}} \| \vd{q}^{i}_\set{J} \|_{x,t}^2} \text{,}\end{equation} For the most part, we report these quantities averaged over the test trajectories below.

elements.sent-2411-308: Next, we investigate how the method performs for non-stationary forcings.
elements.sent-2411-309: We again use the SPOD modes from data generated by a stochastic, statistically stationary forcing, but test the resulting model on a variety of non-stationary forcings.
elements.sent-2411-310: The forcing is active in the same spatial region as before, and is given in this region by \begin{equation}f(x,t) = f(t) \sqrt{\exp[-(x - \overline{x})^2]} \text{,}\end{equation} The forcing is again strongest at $\overline{x = 10$.}
elements.sent-2411-311: We test four choices of the function $f(t)$: a periodic function, a pulse function, a quasiperiodic function, and a series of pulses, steps, and quasiperiodic functions.
elements.sent-2411-312: The initial condition is zero in each case.
elements.sent-2411-313: These functions are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:diff_forcings , and the relative error of SPOD-PG and POG-G as well as the respective projection errors are shown below.}
elements.sent-2411-314: The salient takeaways are the same as in the stochastic forcing case: SPOD-PG achieves substantially lower error than POD-G and than the POD projection error, and nearly achieves the SPOD projection error.
elements.sent-2411-315: With the periodic (a) and quasiperiodic (c) forcings, the solution at the beginning of the interval is different than the solution at the end of the interval, so the SPOD projection error, and thus SPOD-PG, is high at the beginning and end of the interval.
elements.sent-2411-316: Conversely, because the pulse and series forcing are both zero for long enough at the end of the interval for the solution to decay, the error of the SPOD projection and SPOD-PG method are not high at the beginning and end of the interval.
elements.sent-2411-317: Note that because the error is normalized by the average square norm of the solution over the interval, the pulse error is substantially higher than the others.
elements.sent-2411-318: We also note that none of the forcings here (or in the stochastic forcing case) meet assumption (ii), but this has little effect on the accuracy of the method.

elements.sent-2411-319: Finally, we test the method on a range of Ginzburg-Landau systems by varying $\mu_0$ over the range $\mu_0 \in [0.079,0.499]$ in increments of $0.03$.
elements.sent-2411-320: The behavior of the Ginzburg-Landau system changes significantly over this range.
elements.sent-2411-321: At the lower end, the system is linearly stable, i.e., all of the eigenvalues of $\td{A}$ are in the stable half plane, and the behavior is entirely modal, i.e., the eigenvectors are nearly orthogonal.
elements.sent-2411-322: At $\mu_0 = 0.229$, the value we have used in the tests so far, the system is slightly non-modal --- one measure of this is the optimal transient growth \cite{Trefethen93,Schmid07 , which is nearly $5$.}
elements.sent-2411-323: At $\mu_0 = 0.379$, the system is strongly non-modal, with an optimal transient growth of nearly $200$, but is still linearly stable.
elements.sent-2411-324: With $\mu_0 > 0.397$ the system is linearly unstable, and the dynamics feature a competition between the growth caused by the linear instability and the damping of the nonlinear term.

elements.sent-2411-325: We perform two tests over the range of $\mu_0$.
elements.sent-2411-326: First, we build a SPOD-PG model with SPOD modes from training data at each $\mu_0$ with the stochastic forcing \eqref{eq:results:stoch_forcing , then test the model at the same $\mu_0$ with different realizations from the same distribution of forcings.}

elements.sent-2411-328: Second, we take SPOD modes from $\mu_0^* = 0.229$, then use them to build a SPOD-PG model for each $\mu_0$.

elements.sent-2411-331: Again, SPOD-PG substantially outperforms POD-G and the projection error of the POD basis, and nearly achieves its own projection error.
elements.sent-2411-332: As the system is linearly unstable for $\mu_0 > 0.397$, this also shows the efficacy of the strategy described in Section~\ref{sec:nonlinear:full of diverting some of the linear term to the nonlinear term.}

elements.sent-2411-338: We view this as a strong result showcasing the robustness of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-339: Here, we test the method described in Subsection~\ref{sec:nonlinear:bil for handling quadratic nonlinearities.}
elements.sent-2411-340: We do this by replacing the standard nonlinear term with a Burgers’-type (quadratic) nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-341: We exclude roughly $99\%$ of the interactions, which leads to substantial speedup with no meaningful increase in the error relative to retaining all of them.
elements.sent-2411-342: Even with excluding these interactions, however, the method is slower than using DEIM on the same problem.
elements.sent-2411-343: Our outlook is that using the triadic-interaction-sum method of handling of nonlinearities will be more effective for larger systems than DEIM.
elements.sent-2411-344: The quadratically nonlinear Ginzburg-Landau equation is \begin{equation}\frac{\partial}{\partial t} q(x,t) = \bigg[-\nu \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + \gamma \frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2} + \mu_0 - c_\mu^2 + \frac{\mu_2}{2}x^2 \bigg] q(x,t) + \kappa q(x,t) \frac{\partial}{\partial x} q(x,t) \text{.}\end{equation} We set $\kappa = 5$ and otherwise use the same parameters from before (including $\mu_0 = 0.229$).

elements.sent-2411-365: We have introduced a space-time model reduction method for nonlinear systems using spectral POD modes; we refer to it as the SPOD Petrov-Galerkin, and it builds on our work on the same goal for linear systems \cite{Frame24 .}
elements.sent-2411-366: The approach represents the unknown trajectory by representing each temporal frequency of it with the basis of SPOD modes at that frequency.
elements.sent-2411-367: A number of operators involving the SPOD modes, system matrices, and nonlinearity are formed offline as a precomputation step.
elements.sent-2411-368: The online phase of the method comprises solving a set of coupled nonlinear algebraic equations involving these operators for the SPOD coefficients given the initial condition and forcing.
elements.sent-2411-369: The method performed well on the 1-dimensional PDE that it was tested on.
elements.sent-2411-370: The key result is that, at the same number of modes, the error is two orders of magnitude lower than that of POD-Galerkin, and is substantially lower than the POD projection error --- a lower bound for time-domain Petrov-Galerkin methods.
elements.sent-2411-371: In terms of the two questions outlined in the introduction: (1) the method does recover the encoding of the trajectory accurately, i.e., the SPOD-PG error is near the SPOD projection error; (2) the method does so in similar (and often faster) time than POD-Galerkin.
elements.sent-2411-372: We found these results to be fairly robust: we tested the SPOD-PG against POD-G on out-of-sample forcings, as well as parameter variations, and its error was consistently much lower than POD-Galerkin.
elements.sent-2411-373: The accuracy improvement does not come at an increased computational cost: the method is faster than POD-Galerkin for most numbers of modes.
elements.sent-2411-374: Three negative aspects of the method are notable.
elements.sent-2411-375: First, the first step of the method is to take a Fourier transform of the forcing.
elements.sent-2411-376: This means that if the forcing is not known on the entire interval before starting the method, the method cannot be applied.
elements.sent-2411-377: Second, obtaining the SPOD modes requires more training data than obtaining, e.g., the POD modes that form the basis for many space-only methods.
elements.sent-2411-378: Finally, the method applies to a particular temporal window $[0,T]$, which is set by the block length used in calculating the SPOD modes.
elements.sent-2411-379: If predictions on a longer window are desired, the method may be repeated using the final (or near the final, as the error increases at the end of the interval) state as the new initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-380: This is somewhat cumbersome in comparison to space-only methods, which preserve the Markovian property of the governing equations.
elements.sent-2411-381: On balance, however, we are quite encouraged by the results and believe they give reason for further interest in this method and space-time ROMs more broadly.
elements.sent-2411-382: Two important questions that remain are: (1) For which systems do the solution methods converge? In the appendix, we show that when the effect of nonlinearity is small compared to the linear solution, the fixed-point iteration and pseudotime stepping method converge, and that in the opposite limit, they do not for a broad class of nonlinearities.
elements.sent-2411-383: We are optimistic about the convergence for systems in which linear methods can be used to predict nonlinear behavior, as is the case in many fluid dynamic problems.
elements.sent-2411-384: This problem does not have strong modal behavior, which likely makes these approximations less accurate, so we expect they will hold sufficiently in larger problems, especially ones with modal behavior.

\vd{n}(\vd{q}_1) - \vd{n}(\vd{q}_2) \right] $ \Comment{Symmetric bilinear form} \State $\set{N}_k \gets \{ \}$ \For{$l \in \set{K}$} \For {$m \in \{ 1, \dots , r_l \}$} \State $i = k-l \ (\text{mod} \ N_\omega)$ \Comment{Index such that $\omega_i + \omega_l = \omega_k$} \For {$n \in \{ 1, \dots , r_i \}$} \State $\tilde{\vd{n}} \gets \SPOD{k}^{*} \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k \td{V}_k^g \td{\Sigma}_k^{g-1} \td{U}_k^{g*} \vd{b}(\vd{\psi}_{k,m},\vd{\psi}_{i,n}) $ \If{$\|\tilde{\vd{n}} \|^2 \lambda_{k,m} \lambda_{i,n} > \epsilon$} \Comment{Checking if interaction meets threshold} \State $\vd{n}_{klmn} \gets \tilde{\vd{n}}$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\vd{m}_{klmn} \gets \td{\Phi}^* \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k \td{V}_k^g \td{\Sigma}_k^{g-1} \td{U}_k^{g*} \vd{b}(\vd{\psi}_{k,m},\vd{\psi}_{i,n})$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\set{N}_k \gets \set{N}_k \cup (l,m,n)$ \Comment{Updating list of interactions} \EndIf \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\td{Q}^t$, time series data; $\Delta t$, time step used in data; $N_\omega$, number of time steps desired for solution blocks; $\vd{n}$, nonlinear function; $p_1$, number of modes to use in intermediary basis; $p_2$ (if DEIM is used), number of sample points to use in DEIM approximation; $r$, average number of modes per frequency for ROM; $\td{A}$, $\td{B}$, system matrices; $\td{W}$, weight matrix; $\epsilon$ (if triadic interactions are used), tolerance for inclusion. Outputs: $\td{\Phi}$, intermediary basis; $\td{J}_\set{K}$, $\td{J}$ operators at all frequencies; $\td{E}_\set{K}$, $\td{E}$ operators at all frequencies ; $\td{H}_\set{K}$, $\td{H}$ operators at all frequencies. If using DEIM, $\td{S}_\set{K}$, the sampling operators at all frequencies; $\td{N}_\set{K}$, the first set of matrices hyper-reduction matrices; $\td{M}_\set{K}$ the second set of hyper-reduction matrices. Otherwise, $\set{N}_\set{K}$, the set $\set{N}$ at all frequencies; $\vd{n}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$; $\vd{m}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$.
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{SPOD Petrov-Galerkin method (online, constant term)}\label{alg:online_const} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\vdh{f}_\set{K} = FFT_\set{K}(\vd{f}_\set{J})$ \Comment{FFT of forcing} \State $\vd{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \td{\Phi}^*\td{W} \vd{q}_0$ \Comment{The initial condition in the intermediary basis} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{0}_{p_1 \times 1}$ \Comment{Initializing the forcing sum term} \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} + \frac{1}{N_\omega}\td{J}_k \vdh{f}_k$ \Comment{Influence of each frequency on forcing sum} \EndFor \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{c}_k \gets \td{E}_k \vdh{f}_k + \td{H}_k\left( \vd{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} - \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \right) $ \Comment{Constructing each frequency of the constant term} \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\vd{q}_0$, the initial condition; $\td{\Phi}$, intermediary basis (POD modes); $\vd{W}$, weight matrix; $\vd{f}_\set{J}$, the forcing on the temporal grid; $\td{J}_\set{K}$, $\td{J}$ operators at all frequencies (defined in \ref{eq:Lin:Jdef}); $\td{E}_\set{K}$, $\td{E}$ operators at all frequencies (defined in \eqref{eq:Lin:Edef}); $\td{H}_\set{K}$, $\td{H}$ operators at all frequencies (defined below \ref{eq:Lin:ROMfinal}). Outputs: $\vd{c}_\set{K}$, all frequency components of the constant term.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{SPOD Petrov-Galerkin method (online, non-quadratic nonlinear term)}\label{alg:online_nonquad} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vdt{q}^s_k \gets \td{S}_k \vdt{a}_k$ \Comment{Getting sample points in frequency domain} \EndFor \State $\vd{q}_\set{J}^s = IFFT( \vdt{q}_\set{K}^s )$ \Comment{Sample points in time domain} \State $\vd{n}_\set{J}^s = \vd{n}(\vd{q}_\set{J}^s)$ \Comment{Nonlinearity at sample points in time domain} \State $\vdh{n}_\set{K}^s = FFT [ \vd{n}_\set{J}^s ] $ \Comment{Nonlinearity at sample points in frequency domain} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{0}_{p_1 \times 1}$ \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} + \frac{1}{N_\omega} \td{M}_k \vdh{n}_k$ \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \td{N}_k \vdh{n}_k^s$ \EndFor \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{w}_{k} \gets \vd{w}_{k} - \td{H}_k \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\vd{a}_\set{K}$, the SPOD coefficients at the current iteration; $\td{S}_\set{K}$, the sampling operators at all frequencies; $\vd{n}$, the nonlinear function; $\td{N}_\set{K}$, the first set of matrices hyper-reduction matrices (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:deim:matrix1}); $\td{M}_\set{K}$ the second set of hyper-reduction matrices (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:deim:matrix2}); $\td{H}_\set{K}$, the set of matrices defined under \eqref{eq:Lin:ROMfinal}. Output: $\vd{w}_\set{K}$, the approximate nonlinear term formed by concatenating $\vd{w}$ at all frequencies.
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{SPOD Petrov-Galerkin method (online, quadratic nonlinear term)}\label{alg:online_quad} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{0}_{p_1 \times 1}$ \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \vd{0}_{r_k \times 1}$ \For{$(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$} \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \vd{w}_k + \vd{n}_{klmn} \tilde{a}_{l,m}\tilde{a}_{i,n}$ \State $\vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}} + \vd{m}_{klmn} \tilde{a}_{l,m}\tilde{a}_{i,n}$ \EndFor \EndFor \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \vd{w}_k - \td{H}_k \vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\vd{a}_\set{K}$, the SPOD coefficients at the current iteration; $\set{N}_\set{K}$, the set $\set{N}$ at all frequencies (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:Ndef}); $\vd{n}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$ (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:nklmndef}); $\vd{m}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$ (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:mklmndef}); $\td{H}_\set{K}$, the set of matrices defined under \eqref{eq:Lin:ROMfinal}. Output: $\vd{w}_\set{K}$, the approximate nonlinear term formed by concatenating $\vd{w}$ at all frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-415: We propose a space-time reduced-order model (ROM) for nonlinear dynamical systems, building upon previous work on linear systems.
elements.sent-2411-416: Whereas most ROMs are space-only in that they reduce only the spatial dimension of the state, the proposed method leverages an efficient encoding of the entire trajectory of the state on the time interval $[0,T]$, enabling significant additional reduction.
elements.sent-2411-417: Trajectories are encoded using SPOD modes, a spatial basis at each temporal frequency tailored to the structures that appear at that frequency.
elements.sent-2411-418: These modes have a number of properties that make them an ideal choice for space-time model reduction, including separability and near-optimality for long trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-419: We derive a system of algebraic equations involving the SPOD coefficients, forcing, and initial condition by projecting an implicit solution of the governing equations onto the set of SPOD modes in a space-time inner product.
elements.sent-2411-420: We therefore refer to the method as spectral solution operator projection (SSOP).
elements.sent-2411-421: The online phase of SSOP comprises solving this system for the SPOD coefficients, given the initial condition and forcing.
elements.sent-2411-422: We find that SSOP gives two orders of magnitude lower error than POD-Galerkin projection at the same number of modes and CPU time across a suite of tests, including ones that use out-of-sample forcings and affine parameter variation.
elements.sent-2411-423: In fact, the method is substantially more accurate even than the projection of the solution onto the POD modes, which is a lower bound for the error of any method based on a linear space-only encoding of the state.
elements.sent-2411-424: The great majority of model reduction methods use the following two-step approach: (i) find an efficient encoding of the state of the dynamical system, replacing the high-dimensional state space with a low-dimensional one, then (ii) derive a dynamical system for the encoding.
elements.sent-2411-425: Proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) modes \cite{Lumley67,Sirovich87 are perhaps the most common choice for (i), but other choices include balanced truncation modes \cite{Moore81,Willcox02,Rowley05 and nonlinear encodings like those based on autoencoders \cite{Choi19 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-426: Common choices for (ii) include methods like Galerkin \cite{Aubry88,Noack03 and Petrov-Galerkin \cite{Carlberg10 projections, which derive the reduced-order model (ROM) equations from the full-order model (FOM) equations, and methods like operator inference \cite{Peherstorfer16,Kramer24 , which obtain equations for the evolution of the encoding directly from data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-427: Far less common are space-time model reduction methods \cite{Yano14,Choi19,Choi21,Choi21b,Parish21,Hoang22 , which leverage the spatiotemporal correlations present in problems of interest, rather than solely the spatial ones.}
elements.sent-2411-428: With the entire evolution of the state represented in the encoding, the degrees of freedom that represent the solution no longer evolve in time.
elements.sent-2411-429: Step (ii) becomes solving for these static degrees of freedom either by solving a system of simultaneous equations or an optimization.
elements.sent-2411-430: The potential advantage of the space-time paradigm over the space-only one is that solutions to problems of interest exhibit spatiotemporal coherent structures, which offer an extremely efficient means of describing the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-431: In a space-only ROM, one is nonetheless solving for an encoding of the trajectory -- the space-only encoding of the state at all time steps may be viewed as such -- but this trajectory encoding is far from optimal.
elements.sent-2411-432: Indeed, the space-only POD coefficients, for example, are highly correlated from one time step to the next, so they provide a redundant (i.e., suboptimal) encoding of the entire trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-433: To what extent does this persist for a space-time approach? (2) Can the system of equations in the space-time ROM be solved in similar time to the time integration required in the space-only ROM? In other words, does the space-time approach take the same (or less) CPU time as the space-only approach for the same number of degrees of freedom? If the answer to both of these is `yes', then the space-time ROM will be much more accurate than a space-only ROM at the same CPU time.
elements.sent-2411-434: The spatiotemporal basis vectors used in many space-time model reduction methods are separable, i.e., they are formed by taking the Kronecker product of a spatial vector and a temporal one, and the spatial basis vectors used are often the standard POD modes \cite{Choi19,Choi21,Choi21b,Hoang22 .}
elements.sent-2411-435: In most cases, the time dependence for a given POD mode is tailored to that mode, which is accomplished by taking the SVD of a data matrix containing as its columns different temporal evolutions of the associated POD coefficient.
elements.sent-2411-436: In other cases \cite{Choi19 , the same temporal basis is used for each POD mode.}
elements.sent-2411-437: In either case, the temporal basis is obtained empirically.. Refs.
elements.sent-2411-438: Second, various properties of the analytic time dependence of the modes are useful; one of particular importance is that the Fourier time dependence leads to sparser coupling via the nonlinearity than one would achieve with a general space-time basis.
elements.sent-2411-439: Finally, the SPOD basis is separable, i.e., each mode is a function of space multiplied by a function of time.
elements.sent-2411-440: This means that relative to other space-time bases such as space-time POD, the SPOD modes are easier to converge from data, consume less memory, and inner products are far cheaper to evaluate.
elements.sent-2411-441: In our previous work \cite{Frame25 , we proposed Spectral Solution Operator Projection (SSOP), a space-time ROM that returns SPOD coefficients that represent the solution to a linear time-invariant system on a predefined time interval, given the initial condition and forcing.}
elements.sent-2411-442: We showed that this method achieved orders-of-magnitude lower error than POD-G, balanced truncation \cite{Moore81 , and even the projection of the FOM solution onto POD modes.}
elements.sent-2411-443: Such accuracy was achieved at comparable (and usually slightly lower) CPU time than these benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-444: SSOP is so named because it uses the fundamental solution to LTI systems, which can be thought of as a solution operator acting on the initial condition and forcing, and applies a space-time projection onto spectral POD modes to this solution operator.
elements.sent-2411-445: This results in an equation, at every frequency, giving the SPOD coefficients at that frequency in terms of the forcing and initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-446: The goal of this paper is to generalize SSOP to nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-447: First, the operator must be adjusted to account for the nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-448: To do this, we treat the nonlinear term as an additional forcing on the system and use the fundamental solution to the linear system with this additional forcing.
elements.sent-2411-449: Second, the influence of nonlinearity at each frequency must be approximated as a function of the retained SPOD coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-450: Third, the system of nonlinear equations that results from the addition of nonlinearity must be efficiently solved.
elements.sent-2411-451: For cases where this does not converge, we use a pseudo-time-stepping method, which is somewhat slower but more stable.
elements.sent-2411-452: We test the method on two Ginzburg-Landau systems, one with the standard cubic nonlinearity and the other with a Burgers'-type (quadratic) nonlinearity used to test the triadic-interaction handling of the nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-453: We demonstrate good results for both: at similar cost to POD-G, SSOP results in orders-of-magnitude lower error than POD-G and even the lower bound for space-only methods given by the projection of the solution onto the POD modes.
elements.sent-2411-454: We also test the ability of the method to handle affine parameter dependence --- we use SPOD modes from one Ginzburg-Landau system to build the ROM, then test it on a parameter sweep of Ginzburg-Landau systems.
elements.sent-2411-455: In addition to our previous work \cite{Frame25 , several methods have been proposed that use SPOD modes for model reduction.. Refs.}
elements.sent-2411-456: These models are only capable of handling systems in which the solution is known to be periodic.
elements.sent-2411-457: Recently, Ref. \cite{Li25 proposed a nonlinear SPOD-based model, somewhat similar to the Galerkin models in Refs.}
elements.sent-2411-458: Ref. \cite{Li25 then applied this model to find periodic solutions in a canonical incompressible viscous flow.}
elements.sent-2411-459: This latter model can be seen as a spatially reduced version of the harmonic balance method \cite{Hall02 , a method which derives a relation between the various frequencies of a temporally periodic solution to the Navier-Stokes equations.}
elements.sent-2411-460: The harmonic balance method has been applied in turbomachinery problems \cite{Hall02,Hall13 , where the solution is assumed to be periodic with the fundamental frequency given by the blade-passing frequency, and has been used to study boundary layer transition \cite{rigas21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-461: In both, just a few harmonics are retained.
elements.sent-2411-462: All the methods above require a periodic solution, something that might be natural to expect of methods based on the Fourier transform.
elements.sent-2411-463: However, SPOD modes are effective at representing both periodic and aperiodic trajectories, and since the latter is far more common, there is a greater need for reduced-order models for that case.
elements.sent-2411-464: The proposed method, and its antecedent \cite{Frame25 , can handle both periodic and aperiodic problems, owing to the solution operator projection approach.}
elements.sent-2411-465: In Section~\ref{sec:preliminaries , we describe trajectory representation, SPOD modes, and linear SSOP.}

elements.sent-2411-467: We report the results of the application of the method to the Ginzburg-Landau systems in Section~\ref{sec:results , and finally conclude the paper in Section~\ref{sec:conclusions .}}

elements.sent-2411-477: As discussed in the following subsection, SPOD modes can be used to represent trajectories, and a primary motivation for this paper is that the SPOD representation approaches the accuracy of the space-time POD representation for statistically stationary systems as $T \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-478: Spectral POD can be viewed as a `POD of the frequency domain' for statistically stationary flows: for every temporal frequency, there is a basis of SPOD modes that optimally represent the spatial structures exhibited at that frequency.
elements.sent-2411-479: To make this precise, we define the trajectory at frequency $\omega_k$ using the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) as \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:DFT} \hat{\vd{q}}_k = \text{DFT}_k\left[ \vd{q}_\set{J} \right] = \sum_{j = 0}^{N_\omega - 1} \vd{q}_j e^{-i\omega_k t_j} \text{,}\end{equation} where $\omega_k = 2\pi(k - N_\omega \Theta(k - N_\omega / 2) ) / T$, where $\Theta$ is $0$ if its argument is negative, and is $1$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-480: The SPOD modes optimally capture this variability of $\hat{\vd{q _k$ just as the POD modes capture the variability of $\vd{q}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-481: Specifically, the SPOD modes at frequency $\omega_k$ can be defined to maximize the statistical energy captured at frequency $\omega_k$, which is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:SPOD:opimization} \lambda_k(\vd{\psi}) = \frac{\mathbb{E}\big[ | \langle \vdh{q}_k, \vd{\psi} \rangle_{x} |^2 \big]}{\| \vd{\psi}\|_{x}^2 } \text{.}\end{equation} The first SPOD mode at frequency $\omega_k$ is defined to maximize \eqref{eq:SPOD:opimization , and the latter modes are defined to maximize the same function subject to orthogonality with respect to previous modes, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \vd{\psi}_{km} = \argmax \lambda_k(\vd{\psi}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \langle \vd{\psi}_{km} , \vd{\psi}_{kn} \rangle_{x} = \delta_{mn} \quad \text{for} \quad 1 \leq m \leq n \text{,} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\vd{\psi _{km}$ denotes the $m$-th mode at the $k$-th frequency.}}

elements.sent-2411-483: With the modes defined, $\hat{\vd{q _k$ may be represented approximately by a linear combination of the first $r_k$ modes, \begin{equation}\hat{\vd{q}}_k \approx \td{\Psi}_k \vd{a}_k \text{.}\end{equation} Here, $\td{\Psi _k = [\vd{\psi _{k1 , \vd{\psi _{k2 , \dots, \vd{\psi _{kr_k ] \in \mathbb{C ^{N_x \times r_k}$ is the truncated matrix of modes at frequency $\omega_k$ and $\vd{a _k \in \mathbb{C ^{r_k}$ is the vector of expansion coefficients.}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{SSOP (offline)}\label{alg:offline} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\td{\Phi} = POD(\td{Q}^t,\td{W},p_1)$ \Comment{Intermediary basis} \State $\tdh{Q}_\set{K} = WelchBlocks(\td{Q}^t,N_\omega)$ \Comment{Obtain data matrices of each frequency from snapshot matrix} \If{$\vd{n}$ \ \text{is non-quadratic}} \State $[\td{U}^n, \td{P}^{nT}] = DEIM(\vd{n}(\td{Q}^t),p_2)$ \Comment{Obtain sample points and basis for nonlinearity} \EndIf \State $\left[ \SPOD{k}^{N_d},\td{\Lambda}_k^{N_d},\SPOD{k}^{},\td{\Lambda}_k^{} \right] = SPOD(\tdh{Q}_\set{K} ,r) $ \Comment{Get SPOD modes and energies} \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\td{L}_k \gets \left(i\omega_k \td{I} - \td{A} \right)$ \State $\td{G}_k \gets \td{L}_k \tdh{Q}_k$ \Comment{Used for approximating resolvent} \State $\td{E}_k \gets \SPOD{k}^{*} \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k (\td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{G}_k)^{-1} \td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{B}$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\td{J}_k \gets \td{\Phi}^* \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k (\td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{G}_k)^{-1} \td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{B}$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\tilde{\td{A}}_k \gets \SPOD{k}^{N_d*} \td{W} \td{A}\SPOD{k}^{N_d}$ \Comment{Used for calculating $\td{H}_k$} \State $\td{P}_k \gets \left[ \td{I}_{r_k \times r_k} \ \td{0}_{r_k \times (N_d - r_k)} \right]$ \Comment{Used for calculating $\td{H}_k$} \State $\td{H}_k \gets \td{P}_k \left( \td{I} - \exp \left[ \left( \tilde{\td{A}} - i\omega_k \td{I} \right) \Delta t \right] \right)^{-1} \left( \td{I} - \exp \left[ \tilde{\td{A}}T \right] \right) \left( \SPOD{k}^{N_d*}\td{W}\td{\Phi} \right)$ \Comment{Output variable} \If{$\vd{n}$ \ \text{is non-quadratic}} \State $\td{N}_k \gets \SPOD{k}^{*} \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k (\td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{G}_k)^{-1} \td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{U}^n \left( \td{P}^{nT}\td{U}^n \right)^{-1}$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\td{M}_k \gets \td{\Phi}^* \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k (\td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{G}_k)^{-1} \td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{U}^n \left( \td{P}^{nT}\td{U}^n \right)^{-1}$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\td{S}_k \gets \td{P}^{nT} \SPOD{k}$ \Comment{Output variable} \Else \State $\vd{b}(\vd{q}_1,\vd{q}_2) := \frac{1}{2}\left[ \vd{n}(\vd{q}_1 + \vd{q}_2) - \vd{n}(\vd{q}_1) - \vd{n}(\vd{q}_2) \right] $ \Comment{Symmetric bilinear form} \State $\set{N}_k \gets \{ \}$ \For{$(l,i) \text{ such that } \omega_l + \omega_i = \omega_k$} \Comment{Looping over triadic interactions} \For {$m \in \{ 1, \dots , r_l \}$} \For {$n \in \{ 1, \dots , r_i \}$} \State $\tilde{\vd{n}} \gets \SPOD{k}^{*} \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k (\td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{G}_k)^{-1} \td{G}^* \td{W} \vd{b}(\vd{\psi}_{lm},\vd{\psi}_{in}) $ \If{$\|\tilde{\vd{n}} \|_{x}^2 \lambda_{lm} \lambda_{in} > \epsilon$} \Comment{Checking if interaction meets threshold} \State $\vd{n}_{klmn} \gets \tilde{\vd{n}}$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\vd{m}_{klmn} \gets \td{\Phi}^* \td{W} \tdh{Q}_k (\td{G}_k^* \td{W} \td{G}_k)^{-1} \td{G}^* \td{W} \vd{b}(\vd{\psi}_{lm},\vd{\psi}_{in})$ \Comment{Output variable} \State $\set{N}_k \gets \set{N}_k \cup (l,m,n)$ \Comment{Updating list of interactions} \EndIf \EndFor \EndFor \EndFor \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\td{Q}^t$, time series data; $\Delta t$, time step used in data; $N_\omega$, number of time steps desired for solution blocks; $\vd{n}$, nonlinear function; $p_1$, number of modes to use in intermediary basis; $p_2$ (if DEIM is used), number of sample points to use in DEIM approximation; $r$, average number of modes per frequency for ROM; $\td{A}$, $\td{B}$, system matrices; $\td{W}$, weight matrix; $\epsilon$ (if triadic interactions are used), tolerance for inclusion. Outputs: $\td{\Phi}$, intermediary basis; $\td{J}_\set{K}$, $\td{J}$ operators at all frequencies; $\td{E}_\set{K}$, $\td{E}$ operators at all frequencies ; $\td{H}_\set{K}$, $\td{H}$ operators at all frequencies. If using DEIM, $\td{S}_\set{K}$, the sampling operators at all frequencies; $\td{N}_\set{K}$, the first set of hyper-reduction matrices; $\td{M}_\set{K}$ the second set of hyper-reduction matrices. Otherwise, $\set{N}_\set{K}$, the set $\set{N}$ at all frequencies; $\vd{n}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$; $\vd{m}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$.
elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{SSOP (online, constant term)}\label{alg:online_const} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\vdh{f}_\set{K} = FFT_\set{K}[\vd{f}_\set{J}]$ \Comment{FFT of forcing} \State $\vd{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \td{\Phi}^*\td{W} \vd{q}_0$ \Comment{The initial condition in the intermediary basis} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{0}_{p_1 \times 1}$ \Comment{Initializing the forcing sum term} \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} + \frac{1}{N_\omega}\td{J}_k \vdh{f}_k$ \Comment{Influence of each frequency on forcing sum} \EndFor \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{c}_k \gets \td{E}_k \vdh{f}_k + \td{H}_k\left( \vd{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} - \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \right) $ \Comment{Constructing each frequency of the constant term} \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\vd{q}_0$, the initial condition; $\td{\Phi}$, intermediary basis (POD modes); $\vd{W}$, weight matrix; $\vd{f}_\set{J}$, the forcing on the temporal grid; $\td{J}_\set{K}$, $\td{J}$ operators at all frequencies (defined in \ref{eq:Lin:Jdef}); $\td{E}_\set{K}$, $\td{E}$ operators at all frequencies (defined in \eqref{eq:Lin:Edef}); $\td{H}_\set{K}$, $\td{H}$ operators at all frequencies (defined below \ref{eq:Lin:ROMfinal}). Outputs: $\vd{c}_\set{K}$, all frequency components of the constant term.
elements.algorithm-2411-007: \caption{SSOP (online, non-quadratic nonlinear term)}\label{alg:online_nonquad} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vdt{q}^s_k \gets \td{S}_k \vdt{a}_k$ \Comment{Getting sample points in frequency domain} \EndFor \State $\vd{q}_\set{J}^s = IFFT[ \vdt{q}_\set{K}^s ]$ \Comment{Sample points in time domain} \State $\vd{n}_\set{J}^s = \vd{n}(\vd{q}_\set{J}^s)$ \Comment{Nonlinearity at sample points in time domain} \State $\vdh{n}_\set{K}^s = FFT [ \vd{n}_\set{J}^s ] $ \Comment{Nonlinearity at sample points in frequency domain} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{0}_{p_1 \times 1}$ \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}} + \frac{1}{N_\omega} \td{M}_k \vdh{n}_k^s$ \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \td{N}_k \vdh{n}_k^s$ \EndFor \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{w}_{k} \gets \vd{w}_{k} - \td{H}_k \vdt{q}_0^{\td{\Phi}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\vd{a}_\set{K}$, the SPOD coefficients at the current iteration; $\td{S}_\set{K}$, the sampling operators at all frequencies; $\vd{n}$, the nonlinear function; $\td{N}_\set{K}$, the first set of matrices hyper-reduction matrices (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:deim:matrix1}); $\td{M}_\set{K}$ the second set of hyper-reduction matrices (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:deim:matrix2}); $\td{H}_\set{K}$, the set of matrices defined under \eqref{eq:Lin:ROMfinal}. Output: $\vd{w}_\set{K}$, the approximate nonlinear term formed by concatenating $\vd{w}$ at all frequencies.
elements.algorithm-2411-008: \caption{SSOP (online, quadratic nonlinear term)}\label{alg:online_quad} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State $\vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{0}_{p_1 \times 1}$ \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \vd{0}_{r_k \times 1}$ \For{$(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$} \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \vd{w}_k + \vd{n}_{klmn} \tilde{a}_{lm}\tilde{a}_{in}$ \State $\vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}} \gets \vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}} + \vd{m}_{klmn} \tilde{a}_{lm}\tilde{a}_{in}$ \EndFor \EndFor \For{$k \in \set{K}$} \State $\vd{w}_k \gets \vd{w}_k - \td{H}_k \vd{w}^{\td{\Phi}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic} Inputs: $\vd{a}_\set{K}$, the SPOD coefficients at the current iteration; $\set{N}_\set{K}$, the set $\set{N}$ at all frequencies (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:Ndef}); $\vd{n}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$ (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:nklmndef}); $\vd{m}_{klmn}$ for all $k \in \set{K}$, $(l,m,n) \in \set{N}_k$ (see \eqref{eq:nonlinear:mklmndef}); $\td{H}_\set{K}$, the set of matrices defined under \eqref{eq:Lin:ROMfinal}. Output: $\vd{w}_\set{K}$, the approximate nonlinear term formed by concatenating $\vd{w}$ at all frequencies.
